title: Final Project Part 1: Data Import and Cleaning
description: Apply your skills to import a real dataset and clean it for analysis.

slides.title: Project Overview 🏆
slides.content:
# Final Project: Analyzing a Dataset

You've learned the fundamentals of R: data types, vectors, matrices, lists, data frames, control flow, functions, dplyr, and ggplot2. Now it's time to put it all together.

We'll use a dataset about **customer churn** (simulated). The goal: understand what factors influence whether a customer leaves (churns).

Steps:
1. Import the data
2. Clean and prepare it
3. Explore with dplyr
4. Visualize with ggplot2
5. Draw conclusions

slides.title: Load the Dataset
slides.content: First, we'll create a simulated dataset. In reality, you'd read from a file. Run this to create `churn_data`:
slides.codeSnippet:
set.seed(123)
churn_data <- data.frame(
  customer_id = 1:200,
  tenure = sample(1:72, 200, replace = TRUE),        # months
  monthly_charges = round(runif(200, 20, 120), 2),
  total_charges = NA,                                 # to be calculated
  gender = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  senior_citizen = sample(0:1, 200, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.7,0.3)),
  partner = sample(c("Yes", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  dependents = sample(c("Yes", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  phone_service = sample(c("Yes", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.9,0.1)),
  multiple_lines = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No phone service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  internet_service = sample(c("DSL", "Fiber optic", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  online_security = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  online_backup = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  device_protection = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  tech_support = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  streaming_tv = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  streaming_movies = sample(c("Yes", "No", "No internet service"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  contract = sample(c("Month-to-month", "One year", "Two year"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  paperless_billing = sample(c("Yes", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  payment_method = sample(c("Electronic check", "Mailed check", "Bank transfer", "Credit card"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  churn = sample(c("Yes", "No"), 200, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.27, 0.73))
)
# Calculate total_charges as monthly_charges * tenure
churn_data$total_charges <- churn_data$monthly_charges * churn_data$tenure
head(churn_data)

quizData.question: Why is it important to clean and explore data before analysis?

options.text: To find errors, missing values, and understand the structure

quizData.explanation: Data cleaning ensures quality and prevents wrong conclusions.

slides.title: Task 1: Inspect the Data
slides.content:
Use `str()`, `summary()`, `head()`, and `glimpse()` to understand the data. Look for:
- Missing values (NA)
- Incorrect data types (e.g., numbers as characters)
- Levels of categorical variables
- Outliers in numeric columns

slides.title: Inspect
slides.codeSnippet:
library(dplyr)
str(churn_data)
summary(churn_data)
# Check for NAs
colSums(is.na(churn_data))

slides.title: Task 2: Clean the Data
slides.content:
Based on inspection:
- If any column has NAs, decide whether to remove or impute.
- Convert appropriate columns to factors (especially for modeling).
- Create new derived columns if needed (e.g., tenure groups).

We'll do some cleaning in the next lesson. For now, note any issues.
